content:
6 min read
Just now

--

One apostrophe in a filename can kill a production script. Here’s the 1977 parsing rule to blame.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

On July 11, 2026, GNU findutils shipped version 4.11.0, its first stable release in 25 months. One line in the notes closes a thirty-year argument: the 2024 revision of POSIX (IEEE Std 1003.1–2024) finally standardized find -print0 and xargs -0. The pattern that half the shell one-liners on the internet depend on was, formally, a vendor extension until two years ago.
That fact breaks a comfortable assumption: that find | xargs has always been blessed, portable Unix. It hasn't been. And the second assumption, that the pipe makes everything compose with everything, is broken more often, and more silently.
Two interfaces that never spoke to each other
The pipe feels universal because the demos are always grep, sort, wc: filters that read standard input. But a large share of the commands you actually need in a pipeline don't read stdin at all. rm, cp, chmod, kill, mkdir, git revert take their work as arguments. Pipe a list of files into rm and rm sits there, deaf, then exits having deleted nothing.
